title: Gas vs electric heating: which is cheaper?
description: Gas costs 7.33p per kWh against 26.11p for electricity, but efficiency changes the maths. Real UK figures for boilers, electric heaters and heat pumps.
blurb: Gas is 3.6 times cheaper per unit, yet electric heating still wins in some homes. The full cost breakdown.
lede: Ask which fuel is cheaper for heating a UK home and you get a number that looks like a knockout: mains gas costs 7.33p per kWh under the current price cap, while electricity costs 26.11p. That is a ratio of about 3.6 to 1 in favour of gas. But raw unit price is only half the story. Boilers waste some of the energy they burn, electric heaters waste almost none, heat pumps multiply every unit they use, and the two fuels carry different standing charges. Once you account for all of that, the answer becomes more interesting than the headline ratio suggests, and for some homes the cheapest option is electric after all.
short_answer: For whole-house heating, mains gas is usually cheaper. A modern gas boiler delivers heat at roughly 8.1p per kWh, while standard electric heaters deliver it at 26.11p, more than three times the cost. The exceptions are heat pumps, which can match or beat gas at 6.5p to 8.7p per kWh of heat, and homes with no gas supply, where careful use of off-peak electric tariffs narrows the gap. Figures use the Ofgem price cap for July to September 2026.

sections.h2: Why gas wins on raw unit price
sections.html:
Under the Ofgem price cap for July to September 2026, a typical direct-debit customer in Great Britain pays 7.33p per kWh for gas and 26.11p per kWh for electricity. Electricity is about 3.6 times more expensive per unit of energy, and that ratio has held fairly steady for years, because every unit of grid electricity carries generation costs, network costs and policy levies that raw gas does not.

That gap matters enormously for heating, because heating is by far the biggest energy load in most UK homes. Space heating and hot water typically account for around three quarters of household energy use. A typical three-bedroom house needs somewhere in the region of 10,000 kWh of heat each year, sometimes much more in an older, poorly insulated property. Multiply a demand that size by a 3.6 times price difference and you can see why nobody rips out a working gas boiler to fit panel heaters.

But the unit rate is the price of the fuel, not the price of the heat. To compare fairly, you need to know how much useful heat each system squeezes out of every unit it consumes.

sections.h2: Efficiency changes the maths: cost per kWh of actual heat
sections.html:
No gas boiler converts 100% of the gas it burns into heat in your radiators. A modern condensing boiler running well manages around 90%, and that figure depends partly on running it at a sensible flow temperature. Older condensing boilers sit nearer 85%, and a pre-2005 non-condensing boiler may only manage 70%.

Electric resistance heaters, by contrast, are effectively 100% efficient at the point of use. Every kWh you pay for becomes a kWh of heat in the room. That sounds great until you remember each of those kWh costs 3.6 times as much.

Heat pumps break the pattern entirely. Because they move heat from outside rather than generating it, they deliver more heat energy than the electricity they consume. A typical air source installation achieves a seasonal efficiency (SCOP) of around 3.0, meaning 300%, and a well designed system can reach 4.0.

Here is what that does to the real cost of heat:

Heating systemEfficiencyCost per kWh of heatCost for 10,000 kWh of heat a year
Modern condensing gas boiler90%8.1p£814
Older condensing gas boiler85%8.6p£862
Non-condensing gas boiler (pre-2005)70%10.5p£1,047
Electric panel heaters, electric radiators or electric boiler100%26.1p£2,611
Air source heat pump (typical installation)SCOP 3.08.7p£870
Air source heat pump (well designed system)SCOP 4.06.5p£653

At 7.33p per kWh for gas and 26.11p per kWh for electricity, Ofgem price cap July to September 2026, GB direct-debit average. Annual figures exclude standing charges.

The pattern is clear. Standard electric heating costs roughly three times as much as a decent gas boiler for the same warmth. A typical heat pump is in the same band as gas, and a good one is cheaper than gas, all at standard electricity rates before any time-of-use tariff savings.

sections.h2: Standing charges: the hidden line on the comparison
sections.html:
Unit rates are not the only cost. Under the same price cap period, the average electricity standing charge is 57.19p per day (£209 a year) and the gas standing charge is 29.04p per day (£106 a year). You pay these regardless of how much energy you use.

This cuts two ways:

  • A dual-fuel home pays both, about £315 a year before using a single unit. The gas standing charge is, in effect, part of the cost of having gas heating at all.
  • An all-electric home pays only the electricity standing charge and saves £106 a year. If you ever go fully electric, getting the gas meter removed (rather than just unused) is what actually stops that charge.

For a low-use household, the £106 gas standing charge can be a meaningful slice of the total bill. If your annual heating demand were only 3,000 kWh of heat, the gas fuel cost would be about £244 with a 90% boiler, and the standing charge adds another 43% on top. The smaller your heat demand, the weaker the case for keeping a gas connection, which is why well insulated flats are often where all-electric setups make financial sense. Standing charges vary by region and payment method, so check your own bill rather than the GB average.

sections.h2: When electric heating actually makes sense
sections.html:
Despite the headline cost gap, there are real situations where electric heating is the sensible or even the cheaper choice.

Homes with no gas supply

Around four million UK homes are off the gas grid. Getting a new connection can cost hundreds or thousands of pounds, plus a boiler and wet central heating system. If you are off-grid, the realistic comparison is electric versus oil, LPG or a heat pump, not electric versus mains gas.

Storage heaters on an off-peak tariff

Storage heaters charge up overnight on a cheap-rate tariff such as Economy 7 and release the heat during the day. Night rates vary a lot by supplier and region, so the method matters more than any single number: take your night rate from your bill and multiply it by your heat demand. If your night rate is around half the standard cap rate, stored heat costs in the region of 13p per kWh, still dearer than gas but far closer than peak-rate electricity, with no boiler servicing and no gas standing charge.

Heating one room instead of the whole house

If you spend the evening in one room, a 2 kW electric heater used for three hours costs about £1.57 at 26.11p per kWh. Running gas central heating across the whole house for those hours could easily use 8 to 10 kWh of gas or more, but it heats every room. For a single occupant in a large house, targeted electric heat plus a cooler whole-house thermostat setting can genuinely undercut blanket gas heating. For a family using most of the house, gas central heating wins comfortably.

Very small, well insulated homes

When total heat demand is tiny, the fuel saving from gas may never repay the gas standing charge and boiler maintenance. A studio flat needing 2,000 kWh of heat a year would spend about £163 on gas fuel versus £522 on peak-rate electric heat, a £359 gap. Subtract the £106 gas standing charge and £80 to £100 of annual boiler servicing and the difference nearly vanishes, before counting the cost of installing a gas system in the first place.

sections.h2: Heat pumps: the electric heating that beats gas
sections.html:
Everything above treats electric heating as resistance heating. Heat pumps are a different category, and they are the reason "electric heating is expensive" is no longer reliably true. Because an air source heat pump moves roughly three to four units of heat into your home for each unit of electricity it consumes, it delivers heat at 6.5p to 8.7p per kWh at the current cap rate, in the same range as a good gas boiler or below it.

Two things tilt the running-cost balance further towards heat pumps:

  • Heat-pump electricity tariffs. Several suppliers offer type-of-use tariffs with cheaper rates for heat pump owners, either all day or in long off-peak windows. Apply your actual tariff rate to the SCOP method above to see your own cost per kWh of heat.
  • System quality. The spread between SCOP 3.0 and SCOP 4.0 is worth about £217 a year on a 10,000 kWh heat demand, so design and installation quality matter more than brand.

Upfront cost is the catch, although the Boiler Upgrade Scheme grant in England and Wales changes that arithmetic substantially; check the current grant amount and eligibility rules on the official GOV.UK page, as schemes change. For how the technology works, start with our guide to how heat pumps work, and for a detailed running-cost head-to-head see heat pump running costs versus a gas boiler.

For most UK homes with a gas connection and a working boiler, gas remains the cheaper way to heat the whole house: roughly 8.1p per kWh of heat against 26.1p for standard electric heating, a gap of more than three to one. Nothing about plug-in radiators, infrared panels or electric boilers changes that, because they all pay the full electricity rate for every unit of heat.

Electric heating earns its place in specific circumstances: spot-heating a single room, very small or very well insulated homes, properties with no gas supply, and storage heaters running on a genuinely cheap night rate. And a heat pump is the genuine exception, delivering heat at gas prices or better from an electric supply, with room to improve further on a dedicated tariff.

Whichever fuel you use, the cheapest kWh of heat is the one you do not need. Insulation, draught-proofing and sensible thermostat habits shrink the bill on both sides of this comparison before you spend a penny on new kit.

faq.q: Is gas or electric heating cheaper to run in the UK?
faq.a: Gas, in most cases. Under the Ofgem price cap for July to September 2026, gas costs 7.33p per kWh and electricity 26.11p per kWh. After boiler losses, a modern gas boiler delivers heat at about 8.1p per kWh, against 26.1p for standard electric heaters. Heat pumps are the exception, delivering heat at roughly 6.5p to 8.7p per kWh.

faq.q: Why is electricity so much more expensive than gas per kWh?
faq.a: Most UK electricity pricing reflects the cost of generating it, often by burning gas in power stations at well under 100% efficiency, plus network costs and policy levies that are loaded more heavily onto electricity bills than gas bills. The result is a unit rate around 3.6 times the gas rate.

faq.q: Is it cheaper to use an electric heater than gas central heating?
faq.a: Only when you are heating one room for limited periods. A 2 kW electric heater costs about 52p per hour at 26.11p per kWh, which can beat firing up gas central heating for the whole house if you only need one warm room. Heating several rooms or running it for long periods, gas central heating works out cheaper.

faq.q: Are heat pumps really cheaper than gas heating?
faq.a: They can be. A typical air source heat pump with a seasonal efficiency of 3.0 delivers heat at about 8.7p per kWh at the current cap rate, close to a good gas boiler, and a well designed system at 4.0 drops to about 6.5p, which is cheaper than gas. Dedicated heat pump tariffs can lower the running cost further.

faq.q: Does going all-electric save the gas standing charge?
faq.a: Yes. The gas standing charge averages 29.04p per day, about £106 a year, under the July to September 2026 cap. You stop paying it once the gas supply is removed, not merely unused, so arrange meter removal with your supplier if you disconnect from gas entirely.
